informal: Prove that the union of two subspaces of $V$ is a subspace of $V$ if and only if one of the subspaces is contained in the other.
lean:
theorem Axler_exercise_1_9 {F V : Type*} [AddCommGroup V] [Field F]
  [Module F V] (U W : Submodule F V):
  (∃ U' : Submodule F V, U'.carrier = ↑U ∪ ↑W) ↔ (U ≤ W ∨ W ≤ U)
reasoning: The informal claim states: U ∪ W is a subspace ⟺ U ⊆ W ∨ W ⊆ U. The Lean statement encodes "U ∪ W is a subspace" as "there exists a submodule U' whose carrier equals U ∪ W". This is logically equivalent to U ∪ W being a subspace: if such U' exists, then U ∪ W is the carrier of a submodule (hence a subspace), and conversely if U ∪ W is a subspace one can take U' to be that submodule. The right-hand side U ≤ W ∨ W ≤ U is exactly "one of the subspaces is contained in the other". Hypotheses (V an F-vector space) match standard reading. So the encoding is faithful.
